question.text: Suresh has a ULIP policy that has been in force for 4 years and 6 months. He urgently needs funds. Which of the following statements is correct regarding his ULIP?

answer.text: He can make partial withdrawals as the policy has completed 4 years
He can surrender the policy without any surrender charges
He cannot make partial withdrawals as the 5-year lock-in period has not been completed
He can surrender the policy but will receive only 50% of the fund value

explanation.text: **The Logic:** ULIP regulations mandate a lock-in period of 5 years. Partial withdrawals are allowed only after the completion of the 5-year lock-in period. Since Suresh's policy is only 4 years and 6 months old, he cannot make partial withdrawals yet. **The Trap:** Option B is wrong because no surrender charges apply only after 5 policy years, not before. Option A incorrectly assumes 4 years is sufficient. These confuse the partial withdrawal timeline with surrender charge provisions. **Cross-Exam Context:** ULIP lock-in provisions are tested in NISM Series X-B, Series V-A (Mutual Fund), and IRDA exams. Comparing ULIPs with mutual funds' exit loads is a common exam theme. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When clients buy ULIPs, clearly explain the 5-year lock-in. Unlike mutual funds (which may have exit loads of 1-2 years), ULIPs lock funds for 5 years. This makes ULIPs unsuitable for short-term liquidity needs.
